message.content:
You are an adversarial production-readiness assessor for the hc-assay engine at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (a dataset-agnostic, data-sovereign empirical-ML/methodology engine; optional backends are import-guarded; transcripts are committed as provenance and scrubbed of operator PII intentionally). This codebase has already survived SEVEN hardening passes — so easy findings are gone. Report ONLY genuine, concrete, FALSIFIABLE production-readiness defects with a real failure mode; no style nits, no speculative "could be nicer". Each finding needs a file:line/symbol location and a claim someone could disprove by reading the code. Prefer depth over breadth. If a dimension is genuinely clean, return few or zero findings — do NOT invent. Read the actual code (use Read/Grep/Bash; run tests if useful — note: use "python -m pytest", and "source .venv/bin/activate").

YOUR DIMENSION: ops/packaging/supply-chain.
Packaging (pyproject, wheel contents, ADR-0006 dependency-free core), lockfile/hash integrity, CI gates completeness (can a job pass vacuously?), the license gate, SBOM, version pinning, reproducibility, dependency bounds.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	# Least privilege (#H-008): the default GITHUB_TOKEN can be repo-write; this workflow only reads
9	# the code and uploads artifacts, so pin the token to read-only at the top level. Any job needing
10	# more must opt in explicitly with its own `permissions:`.
11	permissions:
12	  contents: read
13	
14	jobs:
15	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
16	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
17	  core:
18	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
19	    strategy:
20	      matrix:
21	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
22	    steps:
23	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
24	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
25	        with:
26	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
27	      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
28	        run: |
29	          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
30	          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
31	          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
32	          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
33	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
34	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
35	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
36	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
37	      - name: Lint (ruff)
38	        run: ruff check src tests
39	      - name: Format check (ruff)
40	        run: ruff format --check src tests
41	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
42	        run: mypy
43	      - name: Test (pytest)
44	        run: pytest -q
45	
46	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
47	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
48	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
49	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
50	  # e2e) run for real.
51	  integration:
52	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
53	    services:
54	      postgres:
55	        image: postgres:16
56	        env:
57	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
58	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
59	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
60	        options: >-
61	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
62	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
63	      qdrant:
64	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
65	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
66	    env:
67	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
68	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/assay
69	    steps:
70	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
71	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
72	        with:
73	          python-version: "3.12"
74	      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
75	        run: |
76	          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
77	          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
78	          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
79	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
80	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
81	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
82	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
83	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
84	        run: mypy
85	      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
86	        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
87	        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
88	        # actually exercising the backends. Fail loudly here if either service isn't up, so the
89	        # gate cannot pass vacuously.
90	        run: |
91	          set -euo pipefail
92	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
93	            pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -U postgres && break || sleep 2
94	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Postgres service never became ready"; exit 1; }
95	          done
96	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
97	            curl -fsS http://localhost:6333/readyz && break || sleep 2
98	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Qdrant service never became ready"; exit 1; }
99	          done
100	          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
101	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
102	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
103	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
104	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
105	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
106	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
107	
108	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
109	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
110	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
111	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
112	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
113	  audit:
114	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
115	    steps:
116	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
117	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
118	        with:
119	          python-version: "3.12"
120	      - name: Install pip-audit
121	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
122	        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
123	        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
124	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
125	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
126	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
127	        run: |
128	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
129	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
130	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
131	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
132	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
133	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
134	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
135	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
136	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
137	          # stay in sync too.
138	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
139	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
140	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
141	          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
142	          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
143	          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
144	          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
145	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
146	            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\\neditables\\n') --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock"; exit 1; }
147	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
148	        run: |
149	          set -euo pipefail
150	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
151	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
152	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
153	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
154	          IGNORES=()
155	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
156	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
157	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
158	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
159	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
160	          fi
161	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
162	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
163	        run: |
164	          set -euo pipefail
165	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
166	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
167	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
168	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
169	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
170	          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
171	          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
172	          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
173	          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
174	          IGNORES=()
175	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
176	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
177	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
178	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")

1	"""License gate (#147): fail if any installed dependency carries a strong-copyleft license.
2	
3	Reads a pip-licenses JSON report (path as argv[1], default ``licenses.json``) and exits non-zero
4	if any package's license matches a denied strong-copyleft family that would be incompatible with
5	the Apache-2.0 distribution. LGPL (weak copyleft, dynamic linking) is permitted. Advisory
6	monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot (.github/dependabot.yml).
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import json
12	import sys
13	
14	# Strong-copyleft families denied in the distributed dependency set. Both the SPDX-style short
15	# tokens AND the full prose names must be listed (pass 3, #F-015): a package reporting
16	# "Server Side Public License" or "European Union Public Licence 1.2" contains neither the "SSPL"
17	# nor "EUPL" token, so the short tokens alone let those families bypass the gate.
18	_DENY = (
19	    "GPL",
20	    "AGPL",
21	    "SSPL",
22	    "EUPL",
23	    "SERVER SIDE PUBLIC",  # SSPL prose form
24	    "EUROPEAN UNION PUBLIC",  # EUPL prose form
25	    "AFFERO",  # AGPL prose form ("GNU Affero General Public License")
26	)
27	
28	
29	def main(path: str = "licenses.json") -> int:
30	    with open(path, encoding="utf-8") as f:  # context manager — no leaked fd (#F-050)
31	        packages = json.load(f)
32	    bad = []
33	    unknown = []
34	    for pkg in packages:
35	        raw = (pkg.get("License") or "").strip()
36	        lic = raw.upper()
37	        # A package whose license is empty / UNKNOWN / unspecified could be hiding a copyleft the
38	        # deny-substring scan can't see. Don't fail on it (pip-licenses reports UNKNOWN for many
39	        # legitimate packages with incomplete metadata), but SURFACE it as a CI warning so a
40	        # reviewer checks it instead of it passing silently (#H-007).
41	        if not raw or lic in ("UNKNOWN", "UNKNOWN LICENSE", "NONE"):
42	            unknown.append(f"{pkg.get('Name')} {pkg.get('Version')}")
43	            continue
44	        if "LGPL" in lic:  # weak copyleft — permitted for dynamically-linked libraries
45	            continue
46	        if any(tok in lic for tok in _DENY):
47	            bad.append(f"{pkg.get('Name')} {pkg.get('Version')}: {pkg.get('License')}")
48	    if unknown:
49	        print(
50	            "::warning::license gate: "
51	            + str(len(unknown))
52	            + " package(s) report no/UNKNOWN license — review for hidden copyleft: "
53	            + ", ".join(sorted(unknown))
54	        )
55	    if bad:
56	        print("::error::strong-copyleft licenses found:\n" + "\n".join(bad))
57	        return 1
58	    print(f"license gate OK ({len(packages)} packages checked, {len(unknown)} unknown)")
59	    return 0
60	
61	
62	if __name__ == "__main__":
63	    raise SystemExit(main(sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "licenses.json"))
64

"""assay_engine — the reusable, dataset-agnostic engine of the hc-assay blueprint.

The single architectural rule (ADR-0002, ARCHITECTURE.md §3):

    The engine never imports dataset specifics. Adapters implement the interfaces in
    ``assay_engine.contracts`` that the engine calls. A clone is: implement the adapter,
    register it, run.

This package contains only goal-agnostic machinery:

- ``contracts``      — the engine ↔ adapter boundary (canonical schema + adapter Protocols).
- ``methodology``    — hypotheses, the three verdicts, the two firewalls, the
                       measurement↔interpretation fence, confirmatory-test machinery.
- ``baseline``       — dataset-agnostic primitives (similarity/distance, descriptive stats) and
                       a determinism/reproducibility harness — the raw material; choice-bearing
                       builders (embeddings, clustering, graph/topology) are supplied per study by
                       the adapter's BaselineBuilder (ADR-0002).
- ``orchestration``  — the analysis graph, phase machine, and governance gates.
- ``reasoning``      — the tiered LLM reasoning seam.
- ``observability``  — self-hosted tracing + experiment tracking.
- ``persistence``    — durable checkpointing, data versioning, vector store.
- ``provenance``     — the append-only, hash-chained audit trail (GOVERNANCE §3).
- ``pipeline``       — ``run_study``: the composed, governed end-to-end runner (ADR-0010).
- ``registry``       — adapter registration.

Nothing in this package may import an adapter module or name a specific dataset, authority,
or taxonomy.
"""

__version__ = "0.0.1"
---IGNORE FILE---
exit=1

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles from pyproject.toml (#G-016).
#
# Dependabot bumps the version ranges in pyproject.toml but cannot regenerate the uv lockfiles,
# so a Dependabot PR will fail the CI "requirements.lock is stale" gate until the lockfiles are
# regenerated. Run this on a Dependabot branch (or any PR that changes pyproject dependencies),
# then commit the updated requirements*.lock files:
#
#     scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh && git commit -am "chore: regenerate lockfiles"
#
# uv is pinned to the same version CI uses (#F-031) so the output matches the sync check exactly.
set -euo pipefail

UV_VERSION="0.11.21"

if ! command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1 || [ "$(uv --version | awk '{print $2}')" != "$UV_VERSION" ]; then
  echo "installing uv==$UV_VERSION (CI-pinned) ..." >&2
  python -m pip install "uv==$UV_VERSION" >&2
fi

uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock
uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock

echo "regenerated requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock — review and commit them."

I have my findings. Let me compile the assessment.

Key findings on the ops/packaging/supply-chain dimension:

1. **Critical/high**: The "lockfile in sync" reproducibility gate re-resolves against live PyPI with no `--exclude-newer`, so it's non-deterministic and currently FAILING (requirements.lock is stale right now vs a fresh resolve).
2. **Medium**: `regenerate_lockfiles.sh` (the documented remediation) doesn't regenerate `requirements-build.lock`, which CI gates.

findings.title: Lockfile-sync "reproducibility guard" re-resolves against live PyPI (no --exclude-newer); already failing on a clean checkout
findings.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml:126-146 (audit job, "Lockfiles are in sync" step) + scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh:31-33
findings.claim: The gate runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes` and diffs the result against the committed requirements.lock. Because most pins are transitive and pyproject uses ranges (e.g. mlflow>=3.1,<4, arize-phoenix>=17,<18), and there is no `--exclude-newer <timestamp>` / resolution-date pin anywhere, `uv pip compile` resolves each transitive dep to the NEWEST version satisfying the range at the moment CI runs. The committed lock is therefore compared against a freshly re-resolved lock that picks up any upstream release published since the lock was committed. I reproduced this: on a clean checkout today the gate FAILS — committed requirements.lock pins arize-phoenix==17.6.0, certifi==2026.5.20, mlflow==3.13.0, mlflow-skinny/tracing==3.13.0, tqdm==4.68.2, whereas a fresh `uv pip compile` resolves arize-phoenix==17.7.0, certifi==2026.6.17, mlflow==3.14.0, tqdm==4.68.3. requirements.lock is reported STALE.
findings.why_real: This makes the merge gate (all-checks fans in `audit`) go red on EVERY pull request the instant any of ~218 transitive packages publishes an in-range release — regardless of whether the PR touches dependencies at all. It both spuriously blocks all merges and defeats the stated purpose: a guard meant to prove reproducibility is itself irreproducible (its expected value changes with wall-clock time and live PyPI state). It is currently failing, so main cannot be merged into until someone regenerates the lock, and it will re-break on the next upstream release.
findings.suggested_fix: Pin the resolution timestamp so the regen is deterministic: add `--exclude-newer <ISO-date>` to every `uv pip compile` invocation in both ci.yml and regenerate_lockfiles.sh, recording the date in a file (or read it from the lock header). Then the committed lock only changes when pyproject ranges or the exclude-newer date change, and the gate compares like-for-like. Alternatively, drop the regen-and-diff gate entirely and instead verify the committed lock still satisfies pyproject (`uv pip compile --no-upgrade` / `uv lock --check`) rather than re-resolving to newest.

findings.title: Documented lockfile-regeneration script omits requirements-build.lock, which CI gates — remediation cannot fix a build-lock staleness
findings.claim: The CI audit job (ci.yml:142-146) diffs requirements-build.lock against a fresh `uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\neditables\n') --universal --generate-hashes` and fails with `::error::requirements-build.lock is stale`. The single documented remediation — referenced by both .github/dependabot.yml (#G-016 note) and the CI error messages — is scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh, but `grep -c requirements-build.lock scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` returns 0: the script only emits requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock. There is no committed script path that regenerates the build lock.
findings.why_real: requirements-build.lock pins hatchling and its transitive deps including trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19, which has a frequent (effectively per-release) cadence. When hatchling/trove-classifiers/packaging/pluggy publishes a new version, the build-lock sync gate fails, and a maintainer following the documented `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` workflow will regenerate the other two locks, re-run CI, and still be red with no scripted way to fix it — they must hand-reconstruct the exact CI command from the ci.yml comment. This is a real, reachable maintenance dead-end (the build lock is only in sync today by luck of timing).
findings.suggested_fix: Add the build-lock regeneration to scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh, byte-for-byte matching the CI command: `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in && uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock`, so the one documented remediation covers all three gated lockfiles.

findings.title: Coverage is measured but never enforced — the integration coverage report is a vacuous gate
findings.claim: The integration job runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. There is no `--cov-fail-under` flag on the command, no `[tool.coverage.report] fail_under` in pyproject.toml, and no .coveragerc (confirmed: grep for `cov-fail-under|fail_under|fail-under` across ci.yml and pyproject.toml finds nothing; no .coveragerc exists). pytest --cov without a threshold exits 0 regardless of how low coverage is.
findings.why_real: For a project that has run seven hardening passes and explicitly added pytest-cov to gate coverage (#103), the coverage number is printed to logs but enforces nothing: a refactor that deletes tests or that adds a large untested module would still pass CI green. The gate exists in appearance only — exactly the 'job passes vacuously' failure mode for this dimension. The coverage signal cannot catch a regression because nothing acts on it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an enforced floor, e.g. `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing --cov-fail-under=<current%>` (or a `[tool.coverage.report] fail_under = N` block in pyproject.toml), set to roughly the current coverage so future drops fail CI. Optionally add `--cov-report=xml` and upload it as an artifact for trend visibility.

content.text:
I completed the ops/packaging/supply-chain assessment of hc-assay. The packaging itself is genuinely solid (clean wheel with py.typed, no PII/test/transcript leakage, correct PEP 639 License-Expression metadata, SHA-pinned actions, hash-pinned hermetic installs, a working license gate that correctly catches dual/prose copyleft, and a dependency-free core preserved by the core lane). I found three real defects, all in the CI/reproducibility plane.

Findings:

- **K-OPS-1 (high)** — `.github/workflows/ci.yml:126-146` + `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh`: the "lockfiles in sync" reproducibility gate re-resolves `uv pip compile` against live PyPI with no `--exclude-newer`/date pin, so it diffs the committed lock against newest-in-range versions. It is **failing right now on a clean checkout** — I reproduced it: committed `requirements.lock` pins arize-phoenix 17.6.0 / certifi 2026.5.20 / mlflow 3.13.0 / tqdm 4.68.2, but a fresh resolve picks 17.7.0 / 2026.6.17 / 3.14.0 / 4.68.3. Since `audit` fans into the single required `all-checks`, every PR goes red the moment any of ~218 transitive deps publishes an in-range release, even when the PR touches nothing.

- **K-OPS-2 (medium)** — `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh`: the one documented remediation (referenced by dependabot.yml and the CI error strings) regenerates only `requirements.lock` and `requirements-core.lock`, not `requirements-build.lock`, which CI gates separately. A `hatchling`/`trove-classifiers` release makes the build lock stale with no scripted fix (in sync today only by timing — committed trove-classifiers is 2026.6.1.19).

- **K-OPS-3 (medium)** — `.github/workflows/ci.yml:105-106`: integration runs `pytest --cov` with no `--cov-fail-under` and no `[tool.coverage]` config anywhere, so coverage is printed but never enforced — a vacuous gate that cannot catch a coverage regression.

Relevant files: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/requirements.lock`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml`.
